title: Discrete Fourier Transform: Signals as Frequency Bins

steps.title: Finite data, finite frequencies
steps.body: The discrete Fourier transform takes a finite list of samples and rewrites it as a finite list of frequency bins. Each bin says how much of one circular rhythm appears in the data.

steps.title: What is a bin?
steps.prompt: For $N$ samples, what does DFT bin $k$ measure?
choices: How strongly the data matches a rhythm that completes $k$ cycles across the sample window.
The value of the $k$th original sample.
The amount of random noise added by the computer.
explanations: Correct. The DFT compares the samples against complex waves with whole-number cycle counts.
Not quite. Samples live in the time domain; bins live in the frequency domain.
No. Noise can appear in bins, but the bins are not noise by definition.

steps.title: Edit the samples
steps.prompt: Change the time samples or try a preset. The middle chart shows DFT magnitudes; the last chart shows what happens when only the strongest low bins are kept.

steps.title: Real signal symmetry
steps.prompt: When the input samples are real numbers, why do the DFT magnitudes often look mirrored?
choices: Positive and negative frequency bins carry conjugate information.
The DFT secretly sorts the samples first.
Only the first bin is ever meaningful.
explanations: Correct. Real-valued signals produce conjugate-symmetric frequency coefficients.
No. The sample order matters; sorting would change the signal.
No. The first bin is the average level, but the other bins carry oscillation information.
